http://www.vpri.org/pdf/tr2012001_steps.pdf
(the final report, 2012)

text:
Every new I&S drop I get that je ne sais quoi, knowing that they're our PARC... I just ache for the thing that comes after and on top of all of this goodness (by such good people, even more importantly, may they be the ones to benefit above all)

Part of the feeling is a mildest melancholy that the projects are bespoke by just that much... the proof of concept always proven but the promise-of-concept left a bit stranded

It's heartening to see that automerge survived in the wild (ie, on github), we want that for all of these projects...

I wish that like the e-ink Remarkable/Onyx set would decide to be the next Apple, swoop in and say "these will all come together into the next star trek computer we're making, watch this" (oh yeah and "we'll stick to the local-first, malleable principles because they're right on")

But if this steams on just like it has for dozens more similar projects, that's wonderful too – divine, really

text: Oh interesting, so this is older than Crosscut?

elements.text: Oh interesting, so this is older than Crosscut?

text:
Yes. This project ran before Crosscut, but the essay took a long time to come together.

The Programable Ink series, thus far, is: Inkbase, Crosscut, Untangle, ...

root.text:
Hi everyone,

I’m going to start live-streaming myself using Lamdu to solve this year’s Advent of Code problems every day during the month of December on my YouTube channel starting tomorrow at 18:00 UTC at <https://www.youtube.com/@yairchu/streams>,
and I wish to invite you to come watch and join the conversation!
It’s my first time doing something like this, and I really hope that it will be interesting :slightly_smiling_face:

For those who haven’t heard about Lamdu (<http://www.lamdu.org>) :
It is a structural editor and language quite similar in its vision to Unison, Dark & Hazel,
It’s an open-source passion-project of <@U78TZ437S> and myself (more contributors always welcome of course!), and its goal is to make programming delightful :)
It started in 2011, and was the first to create a structural editor that almost feels like text editing (which we call WYTIWYS - What-You-Type-Is-What-You-See), and was also the first environment where all IDE services are available at all times without syntax errors or type errors getting in your way. Our solution for type mismatches is pretty much what some of you know from Hazel, but slightly generalised, more info here - <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=viF1bVTOO6k>

Cheers! Yair
